metadata.title: Birds of a Feather: Sharing Democratic Values Eases Immigration in a Postmaterialist Society

abstract:
Although prior research has proposed multiple approaches to reducing anti-immigrant discrimination, less is known about whether priming a shared political ideology works. Integrating a text analysis and a pre-registered survey experiment, we study Hong Kong residents' attitudes toward mainland Chinese immigrants. By employing the structural topic model, we analyze more than 4,000 Hong Kong newspaper articles on mainland immigrants between 2003 and 2020 and show that the political implications of mainland immigration have gained increasing attention in mass media, whereas economic and social concerns have waned in salience.

Resonating with this analysis, our survey experiment reveals that when exposed to a vignette priming mainland immigrants' support for democratic values, young adults in Hong Kong are less likely to support restrictive immigration policies for mainland Chinese. We also find that priming immigrants' economic contribution and cultural integration, as well as natives' family history, has limited effects on Hongkongers' immigration attitudes.

paragraphs: According to the International Organization for Migration, cross-border migration has reached unprecedented levels: approximately 281 million people lived in a country other than their countries of birth in 2020, which is over three times the estimated number in 1970. 1 A growing influx of immigrants, as a consequence, has heightened natives' concerns about immigration and generated a more hostile immigration attitude (e.g., Claassen & McLaren, 2021). Amidst the rising tide of anti-immigration voices and border control policies, immigration has increasingly become a socially pressing and politically charged issue in much of the world. Understanding how to improve natives' attitudes toward immigration is central for building a prosperous and inclusive society across the globe.

An extensive body of literature gives insights into the drivers of natives' attitudes toward immigrants. Prior work has devoted a great deal of attention to the importance of economic self-interest (Malhotra et al., 2013;Mayda, 2006;Scheve & Slaughter, 2001) and sociotropic concerns (Bansak et al., 2016;Citrin et al., 1997;Dancygier & Donnelly, 2013;Gerber et al., 2017;Hainmueller & Hopkins, 2015;Valentino et al., 2019) in shaping individual attitudes toward immigration. A fastgrowing literature has investigated various types of inter-group distinctions that could give rise to discrimination against immigrants. In this vein, cultural, religious, and ethnic identities are found to influence natives' willingness to accept immigrants (Hopkins, 2015;Newman et al., 2012;Sniderman et al., 2004;Valentino et al., 2019).

Despite the accumulation of knowledge on the factors driving anti-immigration sentiments, less is known whether having a shared political ideology can reduce natives' discrimination against immigrants. Individuals have increasingly aligned their social identities with political ideology or partisanship, most notably in politically polarized societies (e.g., Campbell et al., 2018;Egan, 2020). Ideological or partisan identity prompts people to date, marry, and confer economic benefits or job opportunities to in-group members, namely those with shared ideology or partisanship (Alford et al., 2011;Gift & Gift, 2015;Huber & Malhotra, 2017;Iyengar & Westwood, 2015;McConnell et al., 2018;Michelitch, 2015). Put another way, ideological identity can be a superordinate identity that overrides other identities. Yet the effect of ideological identity on individuals' inclusionary attitudes toward immigrants remains understudied and warrants further scholarly attention. In this study, we investigate Hong Kong residents' attitudes toward mainland Chinese immigrants. Given that natives are likely to perceive like-minded immigrants as in-group members, we contend that a shared political ideology can reduce natives' opposition to immigrants.

As a special administrative region in China, Hong Kong enjoys economic prosperity and a relatively liberal social environment, attracting a large number of migrants from mainland China after 1997, the year when the city was handed over 1 3 Political Behavior (2024) 46:1887-1911 from the United Kingdom to China (Law & Lee, 2006). 2 Under "One Country, Two Systems," however, cross-border restrictions are still in place so that mainland Chinese cannot migrate freely to Hong Kong. Mainland immigrants who settled after the handover often encounter discrimination from the local community, especially as political tensions between Hong Kong and the mainland have intensified (Cheng et al., 2021).

In this study, we use a mixed-method approach that is analogous to an "exploratory sequential design" (e.g., Creswell & Plano Clark, 2017;Tashakkori & Teddlie, 2009). We begin with a computer-assisted textual analysis, which yields qualitative insights into the empirical setting of our study and explores contextual factors driving immigration attitudes. Informed by the findings of our text analysis, we then conduct a pre-registered survey experiment for causal inference. Put concretely, leveraging over 4,000 pieces of news articles on mainland Chinese immigrants published by more than 40 Hong Kong news outlets between 2003 and 2020, our text analysis reveals that in recent years, the media coverage has placed greater weight on the political tensions between Hong Kong and the mainland, whereas the media attention to economic issues, most notably social welfare, has dwindled. The increasing salience of political concern surrounding the mainland immigration issue in the mass media is likely to reflect a dynamic shift of public opinion in Hong Kong society, which in turn will shape Hongkongers' perceptions of mainland Chinese. Therefore, we expect that when exposed to a stimulus that alleviates their political concern, Hong Kong residents are more likely to accept mainland immigrants.

We conducted a survey experiment of approximately 1,800 young adults aged under 40 in Hong Kong between September and October 2021. We find that when exposed to the vignette priming mainland immigrants support for liberal democratic values, respondents report a significantly lower level of endorsement for restrictive policies toward mainland immigrants. This finding echoes the view that the Hong Kong society has undergone a "silent revolution" (Inglehart, 1977), a value transformation from material interests to postmaterialism, leading people to embrace democratic values (Ma, 2011;Sing, 2010). In addition, we also test the effects of other treatments proposed by previous studies, such as immigrants' economic contribution (Hainmueller & Hiscox, 2010), socio-cultural integration efforts (Alrababa'h et al., 2021;Hopkins, 2015), and family migration history (Dinas et al., 2021;Williamson et al., 2021). These treatment effects are generally not statistically significant, although we find certain heterogeneous treatment effects by natives' demographic attributes. On the whole, our study highlights the role of shared political ideology in reducing natives' opposition to mainland immigrants in Hong Kong.

This study makes several contributions to the existing scholarship. First, while much of the previous work on public attitudes toward immigrants focuses primarily on economic and cultural considerations (for an early review, see Hainmueller & Hopkins, 2014), our study, to the best of our knowledge, is the first to document the effect of shared political ideology, democratic values in particular, on diminishing anti-immigration sentiment. One closely related study investigates the effect of ideas and norms about gender on reducing discrimination against Muslim immigrants in Germany (Choi et al., 2023). Departing from this study, we emphasize the importance of democratic values instead of gender norms. Second, our study on immigration enriches a burgeoning literature that provides extensive evidence that shared political ideology or partisanship gives rise to in-group favoritism (Alford et al., 2011;Gift & Gift, 2015;Huber & Malhotra, 2017;Iyengar & Westwood, 2015;McConnell et al., 2018;Michelitch, 2015). Thus, our study bridges the literature on public attitudes towards immigration and the literature on in-group favoritism based on shared political ideology in a novel way by developing a new hypothesis and empirically documenting the effects of priming shared political values on reducing hostility towards immigrants. Third, the overwhelming majority of previous studies on public attitude toward immigration focus on Western countries (Alrababa'h et al., 2021). Our work not only tests competing theories in the existing literature but also develops a new perspective for reducing hostility toward immigrants in a context outside of Western countries. Finally, this paper has crucial implications for understanding the relationship between Hong Kong and mainland China through the lens of Hongkongers' attitudes toward mainland immigrants. The findings point to the importance of political ideology in reducing Hongkongers' opposition to mainland immigrants, implying that it might be difficult to resolve the tensions between Hong Kong and Beijing by relying solely on economic means.

sections.paper_id: Jiang et al. - 2024 - Birds of a Feather Sharing Democratic Values Eases Immigration in a Postmaterialist Society
paragraphs: Scholars have investigated the economic determinants of natives' attitudes toward immigration. Scheve and Slaughter's (2001) seminal work suggests that people's immigration attitudes are rooted in material self-interest, particularly the concern of job market competition, and natives thus oppose immigrants with similar skill levels. One line of empirical research has provided further evidence of the role of economic concerns in influencing people's attitudes toward immigration (e.g., Malhotra et al., 2013;Mayda, 2006). Nevertheless, another strand of literature suggests that the evidence on such egocentric economic concerns about labor market competition is limited or mixed (Hainmueller & Hopkins, 2014). Of equal, if not more importance, to economic self-interest are sociotropic concerns about the national economy, fiscal burden, and welfare system (e.g., Citrin et al., 1997;Dancygier & Donnelly, 2013). Accordingly, high-skilled immigrants who create wealth for host societies instead of increasing fiscal strain are more likely to be accepted by natives of all socio-economic statuses (Bansak et al., 2016;Hainmueller & Hopkins, 2015;Valentino et al., 2019). Natives with higher social-economic status who will bear a heavier tax burden are expected to oppose the influx of low-skilled immigrants (e.g., Facchini & Mayda, 2009;Hanson et al., 2007). Given the relatively fixed social welfare benefits in the short term, low-skilled newcomers will increase the competition for social welfare (Dancygier, 2010). As a corollary, poor natives are also expected to oppose low-skilled immigrants.

sections.paper_id: Jiang et al. - 2024 - Birds of a Feather Sharing Democratic Values Eases Immigration in a Postmaterialist Society
paragraphs: Political Behavior (2024Behavior ( ) 46:1887Behavior ( -1911 Beyond economic interests, psychology research suggests that prejudice or intergroup discrimination stems from "in-group love and out-group hate" (Allport, 1954;Brewer, 1999;Hewstone et al., 2002). According to social identity theory, people naturally categorize themselves into specific socio-cultural groups based on an array of attributes, and differentiate in-group and out-group members accordingly (Burke & Reitzes, 1981;Smolicz, 1981;Stets & Burke, 2000). As a result, natives' discrimination toward immigrants most often can be attributed to their perceptions of difference in identities between themselves and immigrants.

Much of the work on the common in-group identity model demonstrates the conflict-reducing effect of shared identity; namely, inter-group bias can be reduced by transforming members' perception of group boundaries from "us" versus "them" to a more inclusive "we" (Dovidio et al., 2008;Gaertner & Dovidio, 2000).

A burgeoning literature has shown that perceptions of cultural threat elicit natives' anti-immigrant sentiments (Paxton & Mughan, 2006;Schildkraut, 2011). Theoretically, cultural threats are rooted in the distinct socio-cultural identities between natives and newcomers. When natives observe the different traits exhibited by immigrants, their perceptions of cultural threats are evoked, prompting them to reject these newcomers. For example, cultural cues, i.e., immigrants who do not speak local language, are more likely to evoke exclusionary reactions from respondents (Sniderman et al., 2004). In contrast, the ability and willingness to learn the local language predicts a higher level of acceptance by natives (e.g., Hopkins, 2015;Newman et al., 2012). Other cultural dimensions, such as religion, also influence people's acceptance of out-group immigrants. For instance, although Muslim immigrants may face hostility in Western societies, they are likely to be welcomed by local residents in Muslim-majority countries because of shared cultural traditions (Alrababa'h et al., 2021).

Similarly, whether immigrants adhere to the host society's social norms and endeavor to integrate into mainstream society also influence natives' perceptions of socio-cultural identity differences between immigrants and themselves. Immigrants with a higher level of social integration are more likely to cooperate with local residents and produce better public goods for local communities, indicating that social integration helps bridge the ethnic divide in ethnically diverse societies (Gereke et al., 2021). Moreover, Choi et al. (2019) find that immigrants who exhibit the awareness to comply with social norms in the host society, i.e., against littering, are less likely to be discriminated against by natives.

Furthermore, an emerging line of work argues that shared memory or experience could mitigate anti-immigrant sentiments. Williamson et al. (2021) show that priming a family immigration history produces small but consistent inclusionary effects in the United States where almost all people are descendants of immigrants. One plausible mechanism is that encouraging individuals to consider an outgroup's perspective, namely reminding natives of their own family immigration history, induces their sympathy toward the outsiders (e.g., Kalla & Broockman, 2021). In the European context, mentioning a resemblance between past and present forced migration elicits sympathy for refugees among respondents whose ancestors had experiences of forcible relocation, and this effect is pronounced among respondents living in regions with high historical concentrations of refugees (Dinas et al., 2021).

Other cultural dimensions, such as religion, also influence people's acceptance of out-group immigrants. For instance, although Muslim immigrants may face hostility in Western societies, they are likely to be welcomed by local residents in Muslim-majority countries because of shared cultural traditions (Alrababa'h et al., 2021).

Similarly, whether immigrants adhere to the host society's social norms and endeavor to integrate into mainstream society also influence natives' perceptions of socio-cultural identity differences between immigrants and themselves. Immigrants with a higher level of social integration are more likely to cooperate with local residents and produce better public goods for local communities, indicating that social integration helps bridge the ethnic divide in ethnically diverse societies (Gereke et al., 2021). Moreover, Choi et al. (2019) find that immigrants who exhibit the awareness to comply with social norms in the host society, i.e., against littering, are less likely to be discriminated against by natives.

Furthermore, an emerging line of work argues that shared memory or experience could mitigate anti-immigrant sentiments. Williamson et al. (2021) show that priming a family immigration history produces small but consistent inclusionary effects in the United States where almost all people are descendants of immigrants. One plausible mechanism is that encouraging individuals to consider an outgroup's perspective, namely reminding natives of their own family immigration history, induces their sympathy toward the outsiders (e.g., Kalla & Broockman, 2021). In the European context, mentioning a resemblance between past and present forced migration elicits sympathy for refugees among respondents whose ancestors had experiences of forcible relocation, and this effect is pronounced among respondents living in regions with high historical concentrations of refugees (Dinas et al., 2021).

sections.paper_id: Jiang et al. - 2024 - Birds of a Feather Sharing Democratic Values Eases Immigration in a Postmaterialist Society
sections.header_raw: A New Lens: Shared Political Ideology Matters
sections.header_norm: a new lens shared political ideology matters
paragraphs: While most of the previous studies focus on the impact of ethnic and cultural identities, we argue that similarities and/or distinctions in political ideologies can also alter natives' immigration attitudes. To be precise, natives are more likely to welcome the immigrants who share a common political ideology with the local community. Traditionally, ideology has been understood as a set of coherent issue positions (e.g., Converse, 1964). But one recent approach considers ideology as a social identity. The ideological labels such as "liberal" and "conservative" are not necessarily based on policy positions but are concerned with a sense of group identity (e.g., Devine, 2015;Ellis & Stimson, 2012;Malka & Lelkes, 2010). Ideological identifications are associated with the symbolic meanings of politics which reflect dominant cleavages in society (Conover & Feldman, 1981). As partisan and ideological identities have become increasingly aligned, such identitybased ideologies are closely pertinent to increased affective polarization (Mason, 2018); that is, partisans tend to dislike and distrust those from the other party (for a review, see Iyengar et al., 2019).

A wealth of evidence documented by recent empirical work has shown that ideological or partisan identity has a profound impact on individuals' preferences and behaviors in a variety of contexts. People prefer to date and marry those with shared political ideology (Alford et al., 2011;Huber & Malhotra, 2017). More often than not, service providers accept lower prices from consumers with the same partisanship (Michelitch, 2015). Likewise, buyers are willing to pay a higher price for a gift card from a partisan-congruent seller (McConnell et al., 2018). Furthermore, in-group favoritism along partisan lines also exists in the evaluations of scholarship and job applicants (Gift & Gift, 2015;Iyengar & Westwood, 2015). When it comes to the immigration issue, given that common in-group identity can generally facilitate more positive and productive relations between groups (Gaertner & Dovidio, 2000), it is likely that natives regard immigrants with a shared political ideology as in-group members and hold a positive attitude toward these immigrants.

In reality, people generally have multiple social identities. When identities crosscut, some can be superordinate over others, even constraining and modifying them (Kang & Bodenhausen, 2015). Levendusky (2018), for instance, shows that in the United States, partisans will ameliorate their attitudes toward members of the opposing party when their American national identity is reinforced. Likewise, in the case of discrimination against immigrants, Choi et al. (2023) find that when Muslim women signal that they hold progressive gender attitudes, i.e., they share a similar gender idea with the local community, the discrimination they encounter diminishes. Put differently, respondents give gender identity a more prominent status within their complicated identity matrices so that they regard immigrants with progressive gender ideas as in-group members, thereby reducing discrimination.
Under certain circumstances, political ideology can serve as a superordinate identity that would unify diverse groups with the same ideology. For example, in 1 3

Political Behavior (2024Behavior ( ) 46:1887Behavior ( -1911 the United States, religious and secular orientations are shaped by political identities (Campbell et al., 2018). More generally, individuals are inclined to adjust their religious, racial, and sexual identities to align with partisan and ideological identities (Egan, 2020). In postindustrial societies, economic prosperity has led to a "silent revolution" in which people's values have shifted from material interests to "postmaterialist" values with an emphasis on individual autonomy and selfexpression values (Inglehart, 1977). As such, individuals will increasingly regard authoritarian regimes as illegitimate and increase demand for democracy (Inglehart & Welzel, 2005). In Western societies, such a "silent revolution" has produced a political cleavage along the liberal vs. conservative value lines (Norris & Inglehart, 2019). In a non-democratic society where the demand for democracy is fairly strong, shared democratic values are likely to be superordinate over other identities such that democracy-minded people see each other as in-group members. In this context, we posit that shared democratic values can drive natives to improve inclusionary attitudes toward democracy-minded immigrants, even though the differences of other identities between natives and immigrants remain salient.

sections.paper_id: Jiang et al. - 2024 - Birds of a Feather Sharing Democratic Values Eases Immigration in a Postmaterialist Society
paragraphs: The economic threat posed by mainland immigration has activated Hong Kong residents' economic concerns. On the one hand, the bulk of immigrants from mainland China, especially in the early period, have been less educated and low-skilled (Cheng et al., 2021), intensifying competition in the low-skilled labor market. On the other hand, the influx of immigrants heightens Hongkongers' concerns about competition over social resources, such as social welfare, healthcare, public housing, and education. For example, the Comprehensive Social Security Assistance Scheme (CSSA), a social welfare program that offers financial aid to low-income families, has been the subject of intense debate in Hong Kong. In 2013, many local residents and political organizations took to the streets to express their dissatisfaction about immigrants' entitlement to the CSSA (Lee et al., 2016). In the midst of such economic tensions, we expect that if mainland immigrants are high-skilled professionals who create wealth for society and are unlikely to compete for social assistance programs, natives' economic concerns will be alleviated, and they will be more willing to accept mainland immigrants.
H1 Reminding Hong Kong residents of the contribution made by mainland immigrants to local economic development can increase their inclusive attitudes towards mainland immigrants.

While ethnic Chinese constitute more than 90% of Hong Kong's total population,foot_4 ethnic homogeneity appears not to bridge the divide between natives and newcomers from mainland China in terms of cultural and social identities. Hong Kong residents have gradually developed a sense of a "Hong Kong identity," implying a rejection of a "Chinese identity" (Chow et al., 2020;Veg, 2017). According to a series of surveys, a growing number of Hong Kong residents identify themselves as Hongkongers rather than Chinese. In 2020, for example, almost 50% of local residents identified themselves as "Hongkongers," while fewer than 15% of them considered themselves "Chinese" (see Fig. A2 of the Online Appendix). Hongkongers use social and cultural cleavages between Hong Kong and mainland China to construct "local" and "China." To be specific, local residents in Hong Kong tend to emphasize Cantonese and modern civil society norms to distinguish themselves from mainland Chinese. In recent years, with the influx of mainland immigrants and tourists, the rising popularity of Mandarin has posed a serious threat to the status of Cantonese.foot_5 Meanwhile, uncivil behaviors of mainland Chinese, such as excessive shopping and public spitting, have exacerbated natives' impressions of mainlanders (Chow et al., 2020;Downes, 2017;Wong et al., 2016).

From a different perspective, if mainland immigrants actively integrate into the local community by learning Cantonese and complying with social norms, the perceived cultural and social cleavages by local residents would diminish to some extent. Ngo and Li (2016) demonstrate that mainland immigrants' assimilation attitudes are associated with better socio-cultural adaptation and higher levels of life satisfaction in Hong Kong. As such, we expect that natives are more supportive of immigrants who endeavor to assimilate to local cultural and social norms.

sections.header_raw: Family History: The Great Escape to Hong Kong
sections.header_norm: family history the great escape to hong kong
paragraphs: Most Hong Kong families can trace their roots back to mainland China because their family members or they themselves migrated from mainland China to Hong 1 3

Political Behavior (2024Behavior ( ) 46:1887Behavior ( -1911 Kong. Since Hong Kong became a British colony in 1842, there have been successive waves of migration from mainland China to Hong Kong for a variety of reasons including wars, political upheavals, extreme poverty, and natural disasters (Chen, 2011;Law & Lee, 2006). To escape from the hardships of life, hundreds of thousands of mainlanders strove to cross the border between Shenzhen and Hong Kong, albeit with a risk of death under heavy crackdowns from authorities on both sides. It is estimated that over two million people successfully fled from the mainland to Hong Kong between 1950 and 1980, while a considerable number of people died on the way (Chen, 2011). sections.paper_id: Jiang et al. - 2024 - Birds of a Feather Sharing Democratic Values Eases Immigration in a Postmaterialist Society
paragraphs: The recent decade has witnessed rising political tensions between Hong Kong and mainland China. While the Chinese central government has sought to strengthen its rule over Hong Kong for various reasons, including national security, Hong Kong society tends to resist Beijing's increasingly aggressive intervention and desires genuine democratic elections. More importantly, in the post-1997 period, Hong Kong society has undergone a "silent revolution" (Inglehart, 1977), a value transformation from material interests to postmaterialism, leading people to embrace democratic values and become increasingly dissatisfied with Hong Kong's unequal business-dominated society and semi-democratic regime (Ma, 2011;Sing, 2010;Tang & Cheng, 2021). Using data from the four waves of the Asian Barometer Surveys, Fig. 1 vividly exhibits Hongkongers' value transformation; the percentage of Hong Kong people who believe economic interests take precedence over democracy has steadily declined, and the younger generation, in particular, lean increasingly toward democracy. In part fueled by this value shift, many social protests in the name of pursuing democracy have erupted in Hong Kong over the past decade (e.g., Cheng, 2016;Cheng et al., 2022;Lee et al., 2019), intensifying the tensions between Hong Kong and Beijing. Moreover, pro-democracy and pro-establishment (or pro-Beijing) have become salient political identities in Hong Kong, and the polarization between the two camps has grown more pronounced (Ma, 2015).

Against the backdrop of rising mainland-Hong Kong tensions, this value shift not only shapes Hongkongers' perceptions toward the central government in Beijing but also influences their attitudes toward mainland Chinese. According to a long-running poll in Hong Kong, Hong Kong residents' positive feelings toward both the mainland government and populace have declined (see Fig. A3 of the Online Appendix). More importantly, mainland immigrants are likely to be viewed as out-group members because of their political identity. In particular, unlike democracy-minded Hongkongers, mainland immigrants are actually likely to support the political and economic status quo and vote for pro-Beijing parties (Wong et al., 2018).

With the rise of postmaterialism, a growing number of Hong Kong residents pay close attention to values or ideologies. Accordingly, whether an individual supports democratic values has become a crucial criterion for differentiating between in-group and out-group members, especially when the demands for democracy have been activated by threats to democratic values and institutions (Cheng et al., 2022). In other words, when a sizable subset of Hongkongers forms their identity, political ideology outweighs other identities. As such, pro-democracy immigrants are likely to be treated as in-group members. Given the importance of political ideology, if mainland immigrants signal that they embrace liberal democratic values, local residents in Hong Kong are more likely to welcome those immigrants. 1 3 Political Behavior (2024) 46:1887-1911 H4 Reminding Hong Kong residents of many mainland immigrants supporting liberal democratic values can increase their inclusive attitudes towards mainland immigrants.

sections.paper_id: Jiang et al. - 2024 - Birds of a Feather Sharing Democratic Values Eases Immigration in a Postmaterialist Society
paragraphs: The corpus of news articles in this study was collected from WiseNews, a Hong Kong-based digital repository that contains newspapers and periodicals in Hong Kong. We focus on the news stories of mainland immigrants and mainland visitors. Keywords such as "mainland immigrants" (內地/大陸移民), "Chinese immigrants" (中國移民), "new immigrants" (新移民), and "new arrivals to Hong Kong" (新來 港定居人士) were used to filter relevant coverage on mainland immigrants. In total, we have obtained 4,453 news articles about mainland immigrants (see Fig. B1 of the Online Appendix). 6 These news articles were published by more than 40 Hong Kong newspapers and magazines between 2003 and 2020, with varying political leanings ranging from pro-establishment Wen Wei Po (文匯報) to pro-democracy Apple Daily (蘋果日報) to relatively neutral Ming Pao (明報). 7 We use computer-assisted text analysis to explore the news corpus. We first employ the term frequency-inverse document frequency (TF-IDF) to extract the keywords of the corpus. The TF-IDF considers the term frequency in a document but gives less weight to common terms across documents (Leskovec et al., 2020). Then we use the structural topic model (STM) to analyze the latent topics of Hong Kong's news coverage on mainland immigrants and visitors and trace the dynamic change of each topic's prevalence (Roberts et al., 2016).

sections.paper_id: Jiang et al. - 2024 - Birds of a Feather Sharing Democratic Values Eases Immigration in a Postmaterialist Society
paragraphs: We employ "jieba", a Chinese word segmentation tool, to tokenize Chinese sentences into words sequences. We use TF-IDF to extract the top 50 words from the 6 The media portrayal of mainland visitors is likely to have an impact on local residents' perceptions toward mainland people including mainland immigrants. Keywords such as "mainland tourists" (內地 客/內地遊客/內地旅) and "parallel traders" (水貨客) were used to filter relevant coverage on mainland visitors. We have obtained 3,925 news articles about mainland visitors. 7 See Table B1 of the Online Appendix for the full list of newspapers and magazines. news coverage on mainland immigrants. As Fig. 2 shows, some notable terms, such as society, economy, CSSA, and public housing, indicate that the socio-economic problems triggered by mainland immigrants have been widely debated in Hong Kong news media. It is also worth noting that the political impact of mainland immigration has garnered considerable attention, as indicated by the term election. Next, we use the STM to discover the thematic structure in the corpus. After experimenting with various numbers of topics, we find that news coverage on mainland immigrants can be reasonably classified into five topics.foot_6 Figure 3 visualizes the distribution of topics with our proposed labels across the corpus. Table B2 of the Online Appendix presents the ten most frequently used words of each topic.

To delve into each topic, we use the findThoughts function of the STM R package to search for news stories that are closely related to each topic. Topic 1 concerns the impact of mainland immigrants on local social welfare. As previously mentioned, the CSSA, an important welfare program in Hong Kong, ignited a heated debate in the media about the impact on the government's budget as well as 1 3 Political Behavior (2024) 46:1887-1911 the interests of local residents. On December 18, 2013, for instance, the Hong Kong Economic Times published an article saying that:

The government should certainly help new immigrants integrate into society and provide basic livelihood security. New immigrants also have the right to receive assistance. However, social resources are limited, and the needs of local people must be considered…Local people have strong ties to the society and should be given priority over outsiders.foot_7

Topics 3 and 4 are related to immigrants' education and housing issues, respectively. While mainland immigrants' educational and residential conditions are far from satisfactory, a number of news articles prime their encroachment on local resources. For instance, on January 20, 2014, an article in Apple Daily said:

The continuous influx of mainland immigrants and tourists proves too much for Hong Kong …many mainlanders mistakenly believe that they can settle in Hong Kong and receive the CSSA and public housing. As a result, Hong Kong's transportation, air quality, housing, medical care, and education problems are over-burdened.foot_8 Fig. 3 Estimated five topics and proposed labels by the STM Both Topics 2 and 5 are concerned with political issues. Topic 2 discusses economic and political tensions between Hong Kong and mainland China since the handover of Hong Kong. Hong Kong media, most notably those pro-democracy and politically neutral media outlets, point to a host of tensions between Hong Kong and Beijing, and some even suggest that the root of the problem lies in the difference in their social systems. For example, on March 23, 2014, an article in Ming Pao said: …the conflict between Hong Kong and China is also rooted in the clash of two social and political systems… the anti-mainland tourist movement with the slogan of 'expelling locusts' has won a lot of sympathy from local public opin-ion…it reflects the resistance of Hongkongers to the logic of the mainland's social and political practices.foot_9 Topic 5 focuses on the impact of mainland immigration on the Hong Kong election. According to the laws in Hong Kong, new immigrants have the right to vote after residing in Hong Kong for 7 years. The majority of the Hong Kong media believes that the influx of immigrants from mainland China has benefited the proestablishment camp in elections. As early as November 22, 2007, Ming Pao published an article saying that: It is estimated that after 2004, at least 200,000 to 300,000 new immigrants from the mainland would have reached the age of 18 and have lived in Hong Kong for 7 years, making them eligible to vote. The composition of voters in Hong Kong is different from the past, which is predicted to have a positive effect on the pro-government camp's source of votes. 12In accordance with this analysis, recent empirical research documents that mainland Chinese immigrants are more supportive of the political and economic status quo in Hong Kong and less inclined to vote for the pro-democracy camp than natives (e.g., Wong et al., 2018).

We further explore the dynamic changes of topic prevalence. As Fig. 4 displays, the prevalence of Topics 1, 3, and 4 has generally exhibited a downward trend, while the prevalence of Topics 2 and 5 has spiraled higher over time. The results indicate that the discussion on mainland immigrants' social and economic repercussions has somewhat waned in recent years, whereas increasing attention has been paid to the political consequences of mainland immigrants. Moreover, by leveraging the results of the STM, we use regression discontinuity in time (RDiT) design to examine the impact of major political incidents on topic prevalence. Figure B3 of the Online Appendix shows that after the Umbrella Movement in 2014 and the Anti-Extradition Law Amendment Bill Movement in 2019, Hong Kong's mass media focused more on the political aspects of mainland immigration, such as tensions between Hong Kong and mainland China (i.e., Topic 2), while focusing their coverage less on social and economic issues, such as the impact of immigration on social welfare (i.e., Topic 1). Furthermore, a dictionary-based sentiment analysis reveals that when it comes to political issues, particularly 1 3

Political Behavior (2024Behavior ( ) 46:1887Behavior ( -1911 major political incidents, Hong Kong's media takes a more negative tone than when it comes to social issues (see Figs. B4, B5, and B6 of the Online Appendix). In view of the priming effect of mass media (e.g., Scheufele & Tewksbury, 2007), Hong Kong residents, especially pro-democracy individuals, are expected to become more attentive to the political implications of the surge of mainland immigrants. Likewise, we investigate Hong Kong news media's coverage on mainland visitors. The results are reported in Figs. B7 and B8 of the Online Appendix. Overall, the textual analysis, as a variant of "computational abductive analysis" (Karell & Freedman, 2019), points to the importance of political implications of mainland immigration, which prompts us to highlight democratic values in our theoretical framework and pay closer attention to priming shared democratic values in our subsequent experiment.

sections.paper_id: Jiang et al. - 2024 - Birds of a Feather Sharing Democratic Values Eases Immigration in a Postmaterialist Society
Jiang et al. - 2024 - Birds of a Feather Sharing Democratic Values Eases Immigration in a Postmaterialist Society
paragraphs: The proceeding text analysis has shown that Hong Kong mass media has primed economic, socio-cultural, and political tensions between Hong Kong residents and mainland immigrants, with the coverage of political issues on the rise. Informed by these findings, we examine to what extent Hong Kong residents will increase their inclusionary attitudes toward mainland immigrants when exposed to information that assuages their concerns over economic, socio-cultural and political threats posed by mainland immigrants.

To test our hypotheses, we pre-registered and conducted a survey experiment in Hong Kong between September and October 2021. 13 The research team commissioned YouGov to implement an online survey on Hong Kong young adults' migration and career development. Our survey experiment on attitudes toward immigration was embedded in this survey. YouGov is an international research and data analytics group and maintains a highly participative and representative panel of over 9 million people worldwide, including Hong Kong. The survey's target population is Hong Kong permanent residents aged 21-40 years old. 14 Like targeted sampling strategies used in prior research (e.g., Malhotra et al., 2013), our targeted sampling approach is to analyze a most likely case. As noted previously, the younger generation in Hong Kong generally embrace democratic values so that the treatment effect of shared democratic values is expected to be present in our sample. Given that our primary goal in this paper is to develop a new theory, it is reasonable to use a most likely case to test a newly proposed theory. This approach can help us evaluate the validity of the new theory at the initial stage and its potential for further exploration. We have a representative sample for the 21-40 age group, which is vital for making valid inferences about this sub-population. Given that the younger generation holds the most negative attitude toward mainland China and Chinese immigrants (Wong et al., 2019), if any of these treatments can significantly reduce their hostility toward immigration, we will have more confidence about the effectiveness of treatments in the real world.

We only include the responses that passed the quality control procedures implemented by YouGov including attention checks in the analysis and detailed in Section D of the Online Appendix. As part of the attention check, we dropped those respondents who completed the survey in under 5 min or took more than 60 min (the median completion time was 10.5 min). The sample in our analysis includes 1,759 valid respondents. The experiment consisted of five groups, i.e., a control group and four treatment groups. Respondents in treatment groups were exposed to vignettes that prime mainland immigrants' contribution to economic development, compliance with social norms, support for democratic values, as well as natives' family history (for details, see Fig. 5). Respondents' demographic attributes of the five groups and the balance test results are presented in Table C1 of the Online Appendix. After being randomly assigned to exposure to one prime, respondents were asked to answer two questions that measure their attitudes toward mainland immigration. One is "In general, what is your attitude toward mainland immigrants?" The 1 3

Political Behavior (2024Behavior ( ) 46:1887Behavior ( -1911 other is "Do you agree with a restrictive policy on immigration from the mainland?" Regarding these two questions, respondents were asked to indicate their attitudes on a seven-point scale ranging from 1 (absolutely not acceptable/strongly disagree) to 7 (absolutely acceptable/strongly agree). By comparing the values of the outcome variables between the control and treatment groups, we can estimate the treatment effect of each prime on Hong Kong residents' immigration attitudes.

sections.paper_id: Jiang et al. - 2024 - Birds of a Feather Sharing Democratic Values Eases Immigration in a Postmaterialist Society
paragraphs: We compare the difference in means of respondents' attitudes toward immigration among the five groups. Figure 6 reveals that while respondents in the four treatment groups do not report a significantly higher level of favoritism to immigration than those in the control group, respondents exposed to the vignette that primes mainland immigrants' embrace of democratic values report a significantly lower level of support for restrictive policies targeting mainland immigration (p = 0.058, two-tailed). In other words, when respondents are reminded that immigrants have shared democratic values with many Hongkongers, their opposition to mainland immigrants diminishes.

This finding resonates with earlier studies on the Hong Kong residents' mass support for democracy and the transformation to postmaterialist values in society (Ma, 2011;Sing, 2010). However, it is important to note that such an inclusionary attitude is only limited to immigration policy preference and the attitude toward mainland immigrants does not significantly increase after respondents 1 3 have learned the political values of immigrants.foot_13 In a sense, the results imply that mainland immigrants, even if they are admitted for their democratic values, still have long way to go to assimilate into Hong Kong local communities.

We perform regression analysis for robustness checks. We use both ordinary least squares (OLS) and logistic regressions. Figure 7 presents the results (for regression tables, see Table C3 of the Online Appendix). The first row of the left 1 3 Political Behavior (2024) 46:1887-1911

panel displays the results of the OLS regression with priming democratic values as the treatment variable; the second row shows the results when adding a set of covariates, such as age, gender, education, and income, into the regression model. Notably, the effect of priming immigrants' democratic values is statistically significant and robust. The right panel plots the results of logistic regressions without and with covariates, respectively. We recode the outcome variable, immigration policy preference, as a binary variable, which takes the value "1" for 5-7 on the 7-point scale and "0" otherwise. The marginal effects indicate that priming democratic values can reduce the likelihood of supporting restrictive immigration policies by about 10%. Given that the percentage of people who support restrictive policies is 46%, the treatment effect of political values is relatively large. The findings therefore support Hypothesis 4.

We then assess heterogeneity in treatment effect across subgroups. Figure 8 reports the heterogeneous effects on respondents' policy preference toward to mainland immigration by household income, birthplace, and education. According to the results displayed in Panel A of Fig. 8, in the middle-income (30,000 HK dollars ≤ monthly household income ≤ 59,999 HK dollars) subgroup, the difference between the treatment group and the control group is statistically significant (p = 0.045, two-tailed); However, in the low-(monthly household income ≤ 29,999) and high-income (monthly household income ≥ 60,000) subgroups, respondents in Fig. 7 Regression results of the effects of democratic values the treatment group do not report a significantly lower endorsement of restrictive policies for mainland immigration. The middle class tends to embrace democratic values as a result of rising educational levels and greater occupational specialization (Lipset, 1959;Welzel & Inglehart, 2008). In Hong Kong, the middle class, especially service professionals (teachers, journalists, lawyers, etc.), generally supports democratization, whereas big business people oppose it because they collaborate with Beijing in maintaining political status quo, expecting Beijing to secure their business interests in mainland (So, 2000). Panel B of Fig. 8 clearly shows that in contrast to respondents who were born in mainland China or overseas, Hong Kongborn respondents take democratic values more seriously when forming their attitude toward immigration (p = 0.029, two-tailed). 16 Panel C of Fig. 8 indicates that the priming effect of democratic values is statistically significant at the 10% level among 1 3

Political Behavior (2024Behavior ( ) 46:1887Behavior ( -1911 the high-education group (college or above) but not among the low-education group (without a college degree). Therefore, the treatment effect is particularly pronounced among Hong Kong-born, highly educated, and middle-class citizens who cherish democratic values more than the rest of the population.

We also examine whether the effect of priming democratic values is conditional on Hongkongers' past interactive experience with mainland Chinese. As Fig. C1 of the Online Appendix shows, the treatment effect is relatively large among Hong Kong people who have had a pleasant experience of interacting with mainlanders. This finding echoes previous studies on social contact and immigration admission (e.g., Clayton et al., 2021).

In the final analysis, we further investigate the heterogeneous effects of the other three treatments, namely economic contribution, social norms, and family history. The results are reported in Figs. C2 and C3 of the Online Appendix.

sections.paper_id: Jiang et al. - 2024 - Birds of a Feather Sharing Democratic Values Eases Immigration in a Postmaterialist Society
paragraphs: We have offered a novel perspective for understanding public attitudes toward immigration. We find that when mainland immigrants signal their support for democratic values, Hong Kong residents are more likely to regard them as in-group members and to reduce anti-immigration sentiment. This study is grounded in Hong Kong where shared democratic values have become superordinate over other identities. In other contexts, we may also need to pay close attention to the role of shared political ideology in improving natives' inclusionary attitudes toward immigration, though a different ideological identity may serve as the superordinate identity. Perhaps there is no one-size-fits-all strategy for reducing anti-immigration sentiment; it is likely that an effective strategy is locally driven and locally tailored.

It is important to note that the generalizability of the causal relationship is more important than the precise effect size (Druckman, 2022). As illustrated in Fig. 1, 63.7% of respondents who are 40 years old or younger believe democracy is more or equally important as the economy, whereas 48.9% of those older than 40 share the same view. The older group does recognize democratic values, but these values are more prevalent and firmly embraced by those under 40. Therefore, the causal relationship identified in our sample is likely to be generalized to Hong Kong residents over 40, even though the effect size may be smaller.

As noted earlier, our sample represents a most likely case, which is especially valuable for theory development since it presents an "easy" test to support a hypothesis. We find that the evidence from our most likely case sample is consistent with our newly developed theory, representing the first step in scientific inquiry. After passing this "easy" test, the theory should be evaluated in a "hard" test or a broader context. From a Popperian perspective, we should continually subject the casual proposition to rigorous tests until it is rejected, at which point we may either abandon or revise it.

When we conducted the survey experiment in 2021, Hong Kong's democracy movement was at a low ebb and the tensions between pro-democracy Hongkongers and the mainland government escalated to an unprecedented level. Under such circumstances, one might expect that the effect of priming shared democratic values on immigration attitudes would be negligible. However, we still found shared political values could be helpful in reducing Hongkongers' opposition to immigration from the mainland. One broader practical implication is that, to assuage Hongkongers' discontent, focusing exclusively on economic policies may prove ineffective, given that the spirit of democracy has taken deep roots in Hong Kong society.
